expectations.text: Identifies the missing readinessProbe (not liveness alone) as the direct cause of traffic routing to unready pods
expectations.evidence: "Liveness probe alone only tells Kubernetes 'restart this pod if it's dead.' It does NOT control traffic routing. Without a readiness probe, a pod receives traffic the moment its container starts."

expectations.text: Recommends readinessProbe with failureThreshold: 1 to remove pods from endpoints immediately on failure, not a higher threshold
expectations.evidence: Shows `failureThreshold: 1` on readinessProbe with comment "remove from endpoints immediately on failure".

expectations.text: Recommends maxUnavailable: 0 (not maxUnavailable: 1) with the explicit reasoning that 1 drops capacity to 2/3 and risks downtime if another pod fails simultaneously
expectations.evidence: Shows `maxUnavailable: 0` with comment "NEVER drop below replica count" in the rolling update strategy.

expectations.text: Sets livenessProbe.failureThreshold higher than readinessProbe.failureThreshold so the pod gets a recovery window before restart
expectations.evidence: readinessProbe failureThreshold=1, livenessProbe failureThreshold=3 with note "give the pod a chance to recover (stop receiving traffic) before being killed and restarted".

expectations.text: Avoids recommending livenessProbe alone as a substitute for readinessProbe
expectations.evidence: Explicitly distinguishes liveness (restart control) from readiness (traffic routing) and adds readiness as the fix.

expectations.text: Identifies the missing readinessProbe (not liveness alone) as the direct cause of traffic routing to unready pods
expectations.evidence: "Without a readiness probe, Kubernetes adds the pod to the Service endpoints immediately when it enters Running state — before your application is actually ready to serve traffic."

expectations.text: Recommends readinessProbe with failureThreshold: 1 to remove pods from endpoints immediately on failure, not a higher threshold
expectations.evidence: Shows `failureThreshold: 3` for readinessProbe — not the immediate-removal threshold of 1.

expectations.text: Recommends maxUnavailable: 0 (not maxUnavailable: 1) with the explicit reasoning that 1 drops capacity to 2/3 and risks downtime if another pod fails simultaneously
expectations.evidence: Shows `maxUnavailable: 0` with "Zero-downtime: never reduce below desired replicas" via minReadySeconds.

expectations.text: Sets livenessProbe.failureThreshold higher than readinessProbe.failureThreshold so the pod gets a recovery window before restart
expectations.evidence: Both readinessProbe and livenessProbe have `failureThreshold: 3` — equal values, not liveness higher than readiness.

expectations.text: Avoids recommending livenessProbe alone as a substitute for readinessProbe
expectations.evidence: Adds readiness probe as the fix; does not suggest liveness alone is sufficient.

expectations.text: Provides the exact drain flags --delete-emptydir-data=true --ignore-daemonsets --timeout=360s, not a bare kubectl drain
expectations.evidence: Shows `kubectl drain <node-name> --delete-emptydir-data=true --ignore-daemonsets --timeout=360s`.

expectations.text: Explains that --ignore-daemonsets is required because DaemonSet pods cannot be moved and would otherwise block the drain
expectations.evidence: Uses --ignore-daemonsets but does not explain that DaemonSet pods cannot be moved and would block the drain without this flag.

expectations.text: Includes kubectl uncordon <node> as the post-maintenance step to re-enable scheduling
expectations.evidence: Shows `kubectl uncordon <node-name>` as step 6 after the node returns.

expectations.text: Specifically warns that draining a single-node cluster evicts CoreDNS and causes a DNS outage
expectations.evidence: Warns about single-node drain causing pods to enter Pending state and workloads going down, but does not specifically name CoreDNS or DNS outage.

expectations.text: Avoids recommending kubectl cordon alone (without drain) as sufficient preparation before a reboot
expectations.evidence: Provides full drain procedure; cordon is step 1 of a multi-step process, not presented as sufficient alone.

expectations.text: Provides the exact drain flags --delete-emptydir-data=true --ignore-daemonsets --timeout=360s, not a bare kubectl drain
expectations.evidence: Shows `--ignore-daemonsets --delete-emptydir-data --grace-period=60` — missing --timeout=360s and uses --grace-period instead.

expectations.text: Explains that --ignore-daemonsets is required because DaemonSet pods cannot be moved and would otherwise block the drain
expectations.evidence: "DaemonSets are ignored by --ignore-daemonsets (they'll restart automatically after reboot)" — explains behavior after reboot but not why the flag is required to prevent blocking.

expectations.text: Includes kubectl uncordon <node> as the post-maintenance step to re-enable scheduling
expectations.evidence: Shows `kubectl uncordon <node-name>` as step 5.

expectations.text: Specifically warns that draining a single-node cluster evicts CoreDNS and causes a DNS outage
expectations.evidence: Warns about single-node downtime and PDB issues but does not specifically mention CoreDNS eviction causing DNS outage.

expectations.text: Avoids recommending kubectl cordon alone (without drain) as sufficient preparation before a reboot
expectations.evidence: Provides full drain procedure; cordon is step 1 of a multi-step process.

expectations.text: Identifies resourceVersion as the optimistic concurrency token causing the conflict, not a generic 'race condition'
expectations.evidence: "the resourceVersion may have changed between your fetch and apply — Kubernetes rejects the stale version with a conflict error" — identifies resourceVersion as the specific cause.

expectations.text: Distinguishes the two valid approaches: delete resourceVersion for single-writer CI vs. keep it and retry the read-patch-apply loop when GitOps or HPA is active
expectations.evidence: Recommends kubectl set image as the primary fix; does not discuss the two-approach distinction (delete resourceVersion vs keep+retry).

expectations.text: Explicitly names ArgoCD and HPA as the multi-writer scenario that makes unconditional apply dangerous (reconciliation fight)
expectations.evidence: "No conflict with HPA or ArgoCD because you're not replacing the entire spec" — names both controllers explicitly.

expectations.text: Recommends looking up containers and env vars by name (not by index) because order in the containers array can change
expectations.evidence: No mention of name vs index lookup for containers or env vars.

expectations.text: Avoids recommending kubectl set image as a universal fix, since it does not update annotations like kubernetes.io/change-cause or env vars used for version tracing
expectations.evidence: Recommends `kubectl set image` as the primary fix without noting its limitations for annotations or env var updates.

expectations.text: Identifies resourceVersion as the optimistic concurrency token causing the conflict, not a generic 'race condition'
expectations.evidence: "The conflict error is 409 Conflict — Kubernetes' optimistic concurrency control rejecting your write because the resourceVersion changed between your GET and your APPLY."

expectations.text: Distinguishes the two valid approaches: delete resourceVersion for single-writer CI vs. keep it and retry the read-patch-apply loop when GitOps or HPA is active
expectations.evidence: Recommends kubectl set image, server-side patch, or server-side apply — does not discuss the delete-resourceVersion vs keep+retry distinction.

expectations.text: Explicitly names ArgoCD and HPA as the multi-writer scenario that makes unconditional apply dangerous (reconciliation fight)
expectations.evidence: "This races with other controllers that also modify the same Deployment (HPA scaling replicas, ArgoCD syncing spec)."

expectations.text: Recommends looking up containers and env vars by name (not by index) because order in the containers array can change
expectations.evidence: No mention of name vs index lookup for containers or env vars.

expectations.text: Avoids recommending kubectl set image as a universal fix, since it does not update annotations like kubernetes.io/change-cause or env vars used for version tracing
expectations.evidence: Recommends `kubectl set image` as Option 1 (simplest) without noting its limitations.
